
Let $X(t)=(S(t),I(t),R(t))$ be a discrete-valued SIR model, and suppose that the counting process $N_{SI}(t)$ has per-capita rate $\mu_{SI}(t)=\beta I(t)$. Consider four Euler approximations:
\begin{eqnarray} 
N_{SI}(t+\delta) - N_{SI}(t) &\sim& \mathrm{Poisson} \big(\beta I(t) S(t) \delta\big)
\\  
N_{SI}(t+\delta) - N_{SI}(t) &\sim& \mathrm{Binomial} \big(S(t), \beta I(t)\delta\big)
\\  
N_{SI}(t+\delta) - N_{SI}(t) &\sim& \mathrm{Binomial} \big(S(t), 1-\exp\big\{-\beta I(t)\delta\big\}\big)
\\  
N_{SI}(t+\delta) - N_{SI}(t) &\sim& \mathrm{Binomial} \big(S(t), \beta I(t)\delta\big/(1+\beta I(t)\delta) \big)
\end{eqnarray}
Which (if any) of these choices is preferable, and why?

(A). (1) because it is the simplest, quickest to simulate, and most easily understood. All continuous time, discrete state, compartment models have approximately Poisson increments on small time intervals.

(B). (2) because each individual in $S$ has approximately an independent chance $\beta I(t)\delta$ of transitioning to $I$.

(C). (3) because each individual in $S$ has approximately an independent hazard rate $\beta I(t)\delta$ of transitioning to $I$.

(D). (4) because it is asymptotically equivalent to (3), up to order $\delta^2$, but quicker to compute.

(E). It should not matter which you choose. All the methods are correct Euler schemes, with the same justification in a limit when $\delta$ is small. Therefore, there is no theoretical reason to prefer one over another.
